body:
## Summary

Block-layer read faults (`EIO` / dead sector) are **not distinguishable from transient
faults on the gRPC wire**. A remote D server whose disk faults a *read* surfaces an
`io::Error` with `raw_os_error() == Some(5)`, but that signal is flattened to a string
and collapsed into a generic `Status::internal` crossing the seam — so the client cannot
reconstruct it as a permanent durability fault. This breaks the
permanent-loss-vs-transient distinction ADR-0010 requires to hold "along the whole path
from the store to the consumer's decision point."

Surfaced during Check sign-off of #251 (reconstruction read-around on a fragment read
fault). #251 restores the read-around for **local / fs-backed** D servers but
deliberately scoped `chunkstore-grpc` out; this is the deferred follow-up that makes the
fix reach **networked** D servers (the dominant production transport).

## Root cause (verified on `main`)

Two lossy hops, neither of which carries the errno as structured data (a gRPC `Status`
is only `Code` + `String`):

1. **Server** — `crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/server.rs:83-89`: a non-integrity
   `get_fragment` error takes the `else` branch and becomes `Status::internal(e.to_string())`.
   The `io::Error` (and its `raw_os_error() == Some(5)`) is flattened to a plain string.
   Only `is_integrity_fault` gets a dedicated, reconstructable code (`Code::DataLoss`).
2. **Client** — `crates/chunkstore-grpc/src/client.rs:25-34, :90`: `classify_get_status`
   rebuilds a typed seam error **only** for `Code::DataLoss`; everything else becomes a
   `TransportError` (the transient/generic class). The `EIO` therefore arrives with no
   `io::Error` anywhere in its source chain.

Consequence: `is_block_read_fault` (added in #251, `reconstruction.rs`) walks `source()`
for an `io::Error` with `raw_os_error() == Some(5)` and finds nothing for a remote fault,
so `reconstruction::assess` classifies a real remote dead sector as **transient** and
retries it forever — the chunk is never read around / rebuilt. The brief's headline
scenario ("a disk that goes bad *after* its data lands can never be repaired") remains
unrestored for networked servers.

## Proposed direction

Give block-layer read faults a distinct, reconstructable wire signal — mirroring how
`IntegrityFault` rides `Code::DataLoss` — so the permanent-vs-transient distinction
survives the seam. Options to weigh:

- A dedicated `Status` code (or a typed detail on an existing code) for "device could not
  return the bytes," reconstructed client-side into the same permanent-read-fault shape
  `reconstruction::assess` / `read.rs` already classify; and/or
- Decide the closure of "permanent block-layer fault" at the seam (errno-5-only vs. a
  broader dead-sector class — see #251 §6 item 2) so the classification is defined once,
  on the wire contract, not re-derived per consumer.

## Acceptance

- A remote `get_fragment` `EIO`/block-read fault is classified as a **permanent**
  durability fault by the client-side `ChunkStore`, so `reconstruction::assess` reads
  around it and rebuilds from the >=k survivors — same behaviour as the local/fs path.
- A genuinely transient remote fault (UNAVAILABLE / DEADLINE_EXCEEDED) is still
  propagated to the retry policy (no spurious re-placement).
- Regression coverage over the gRPC seam (not just an in-process mock).

## Refs

- Surfaced by: #251 (Check sign-off, §6 items 1 & 3)
- Related harness: #195 (Tier-1 disk-fault / `dm-error` scenario)
- Milestone epic: #147 (M3 — Custodians)
- Contract: ADR-0010 / `crates/traits/src/lib.rs:64` (`IntegrityFault` seam)

milestone.title: M3 — Custodians
milestone.description: The self-maintaining durability plane (L4): GC, scrub, reconstruction, rebalance + durability telemetry. Makes single-zone trustworthy; gates the Step-2 release. Plan: proposal 0005.

title: chunkstore-grpc: block-layer read faults (EIO) are indistinguishable from transient on the wire — remote dead sectors classify as transient
